text.title: Backup Strategies (3-2-1-1-0) Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Ransomware actor gains IAM access, deletes governance-locked backups; supposedly 'immutable' copies gone.
antipattern.root-cause: Operator chose GOVERNANCE because 'we might need to delete by mistake'.
antipattern.fix: Switch to COMPLIANCE. Accept the trade-off (can't override) — that's the whole point of immutability.

antipattern.symptom: Three years of backups; first ransomware incident; pg_restore fails on every dump because a schema change broke the procedure.
antipattern.root-cause: Verification was 'we'll test when we need it'; never happened.
antipattern.fix: Add nightly automated restore-test to an isolated DB. Pipe success/failure to monitoring. Page on missed verification.

antipattern.symptom: Cloud-account compromise; attacker deletes the S3 bucket holding 'remote' backups; local NAS is wiped by encrypt.
antipattern.root-cause: All copies in the same cloud account; same-region different-AZ counted as 'offsite'.
antipattern.fix: Add a cross-account or cross-cloud copy. Use S3 Replication to a separate AWS account / different provider.

antipattern.symptom: DB corruption silently overwritten in last snapshot; PITR not enabled; only one copy.
antipattern.root-cause: Author thought 'cloud is enough'; only the primary snapshot existed.
antipattern.fix: Apply 3-2-1-1-0 floor. Enable PITR (Point-in-Time Recovery) for ≥7 days.
